summary: Asparouhova argues that major tech corporations like Google and Facebook have become de-facto governments of the internet, providing essential 'public services' that form the backbone of online communication. She notes that while governments provide offline infrastructure like streetlights and waste management, corporations now provide the equivalent online utilities. However, unlike governments, these corporations are legally obligated to serve shareholders, not the public. She questions whether this model is sustainable, especially as ephemeral and anonymous apps emerge that don't fit traditional advertising models.
key_points: Internet services like search, email, and social media function as public utilities but are controlled by a handful of corporations
High-value acquisitions can be reframed as corporations 'sponsoring' services to continue serving society under centralized management
For-profit corporations cannot be neutral governing bodies because their legal obligation is to shareholders, not citizens
Ephemeral and anonymous apps represent a challenge to the advertising-based model that funds internet infrastructure
The question of how to sustainably incubate essential internet services remains unresolved
money_quotes: If government's role is, at least in part, to support the functional backbone services of our society, these corporations have become our de-facto governments.
Companies like Google and Facebook might serve the same purpose online as government entities offline, but they are not - cannot be - a neutral entity. Even if they wanted to be.
It shouldn't ever be a for-profit corporation's job to serve the role of public government.
They sold to a corporation, who would sponsor their service so that it could continue to serve society while being managed by a centralized power.
The next wave of Internet communication - ephemeral and anonymous apps - do not collect data on their users. The old advertising models don't work as easily on these products.
key_insight: Tech corporations have assumed governmental roles in providing essential internet infrastructure, but their profit obligations make them fundamentally unsuited to serve as neutral public institutions.
